title: Bounded Rationality

content.statement: Limit scope to make decisions tractable.
content.question: What are you trying to solve at too broad a scope?
content.life_lens: Change one habit at a time.
sop: Write down every unknown or variable you're currently trying to account for
Rate the complexity of the full problem vs. a deliberately narrowed version (see calculation, page 7)
Draw a hard boundary around what you'll actually consider, ignore the peripheral variables on purpose
Decide within those bounds using only the information available now
Revisit the boundary later if new information changes the picture, but don't wait for it first
content.explainer: Trying to account for every variable feels responsible, but it usually just produces paralysis, because complexity grows faster than your ability to reason about it. Bounded rationality isn't a shortcut for lazy thinking, it's the recognition that a decision made within a deliberately narrow, honest scope beats one that stalls out trying to be complete.
questions: What are you trying to solve at too broad a scope?
What decision have you been stuck on for weeks because you're trying to account for every variable instead of the ones that actually matter?
If you had to decide with only the information you have right now, no more research allowed, what would you choose?

definitions.def: Making the best decision possible within a deliberately limited scope, instead of trying to account for every variable.

content.equation_example: Scope: rate 1-10 how narrow and defined the problem is (10 = tightly bounded). Complexity: rate 1-10 how many variables are actually in play. Scope 8 divided by Complexity 4 gives Quality 2.0, a good, tractable decision. Scope 3 divided by Complexity 9 gives Quality 0.33, an overwhelmed, low-quality decision. Higher quality means a decision you can actually make well; lower means you're trying to solve too much at once.
content.closing_statement: A narrower problem is usually a solvable one.

caption:
Principle Nº 221: Bounded Rationality

Limit scope to make decisions tractable. Trying to account for every variable feels responsible, but it usually just produces paralysis, because complexity grows faster than your ability to reason about it.

Ask yourself: What are you trying to solve at too broad a scope?
